This is an empirical fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity in humans. It used visual food vs. non-food pictures with a within-subject contrast (food>non-food), conducted whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (FLAME1) with cluster correction, and reports whole-brain results in figures/tables. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex (pre-pubertal children, 7–11 yrs), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity.

This is an empirical BOLD-fMRI study using visual food cues (pictures) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity, with within-subject food>non-food contrasts. The authors performed group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses comparing food-cue reactivity after glucose vs. water (FDR cluster-corrected), in addition to ROI analyses. Participant age and sex are reported (19–25 years; 12M/13F), cue type is food, scanner details (MAGNETOM Prisma; parameters consistent with 3T) and processing software (FSL) are provided. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for (I3) task modality and cue type, (I4) within-participant food>control contrasts, (I5) group-level whole-brain voxelwise task analysis, (I6) whole-brain results reported in text/figures, and (I7) participant and methodological details reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only reporting, connectivity-only analyses, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

The fMRI component used monetary reward cues (10 pence) and an incentive-conflict task rather than drug-related or the natural reward cue types specified by the review (e.g., food, sexual) — it does not assess drug or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity. Although the study includes within-participant contrasts (reward vs nonreward) and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise results (with coordinates and thresholding), the cue type and study objective do not match the meta-analysis focus on drug and natural reward cue-reactivity. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (and I7 re: relevant cue type) and must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study of appetitive (natural reward: food) cue-reactivity using visual cues (pictures combined with tones) and liquid-food US. The main within-participant contrasts include CS+ > CS− (natural reward-related cues > control cues) at group level. Whole-brain group-level GLM analyses were performed (flexible factorial), and whole-brain exploratory results are reported (Supplementary) with coordinates; ROI SVC was applied for specific hypotheses but whole-brain task-evoked maps are available. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical). Therefore the paper meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual food-cue reactivity in adults with obesity and includes within-subject contrasts of food > object. However, the manuscript reports ROI-based analyses (COPEs extracted from 14 a priori ROIs) and presents ROI-wise mixed-effects results; no group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map, coordinates, or whole-brain figures/tables are reported in the text or supplementary materials. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and meets exclusion E3 (ROI-only analyses). Inclusion criteria I3 and I4 are met, but I5 (whole-brain results) is not. Additionally, scanner field strength is not clearly reported.

This study is a behavioral cue-reactivity experiment (17 non-treatment-seeking alcoholics) that measured self-reported craving and mood after alcohol vs. water cues and alcohol/placebo primes. No functional MRI data, no neuroimaging methods, and no whole-brain or voxelwise task activation maps are reported. Therefore it does not meet the core inclusion requirement for fMRI studies of cue-reactivity (I3) and related imaging criteria (I4–I7). It should be excluded from a meta-analysis restricted to fMRI cue-reactivity studies.

This study used pupillometry (eye-tracking) to measure cue-reactivity and did not acquire or report any functional MRI data, scanner field strength, or fMRI processing/software. It therefore does not meet the core inclusion criterion requiring a functional MRI study (I3) and provides no whole-brain fMRI activation maps (I5/I7). As such it is not eligible for a meta-analysis of fMRI cue-reactivity studies.

The study is an fMRI investigation of food (natural reward) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports within-participant contrasts (fattening vs nonfattening foods). However, the neuroimaging analyses are reported using an a priori ROI approach and regional summary measures (ROI averages and ICCs). The paper does not report group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or whole-brain coordinates/results; therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting). Other required study descriptors (age/sex, cue type, scanner, software) are reported, but the lack of whole-brain results prevents inclusion in the meta-analysis.

The paper includes an fMRI experiment (study 1) that uses visual cues (cartoon/image of a milkshake) to elicit anticipatory responses to a natural reward (food). It reports a within-participant contrast (anticipatory milkshake cue > anticipatory tasteless-solution cue), and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-level q-FDR correction and reported activation coordinates (postcentral gyrus). Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and processing software (SPM12, FSL) are provided. Thus all required inclusion criteria are met.

This is an fMRI study of food cue processing (natural reward) with whole-brain group analyses and thorough reporting. However, it does not include a within-subject contrast of reward cues > control (e.g., food images versus neutral/non-food images). All experimental conditions use food pictures and contrasts are between task instructions (inhibition, imaginary eating, passive viewing) applied to the same food cues rather than food cues > control stimuli. The meta-analysis requires at least one within-participant cue>control contrast; because that is absent, the study does not meet inclusion criterion I4 and must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study using visual drug-related film cues in amphetamine-dependent participants. It reports a within-subject contrast (Drug movies > Neutral movies) and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked results with FWE correction. Methods report SPM12 processing, 3 T scanner field strength, and participant age and sex (male, 20–65). Therefore it satisfies criteria for visual cue-related fMRI, within-participant drug>control contrast, whole-brain task activation reporting, acceptable reporting formats, and required participant/scanner/method details.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual Pavlovian cues (colored lights and snack images) paired with food rewards (natural rewards). The study reports within-participant contrasts of reward versus control cues (e.g., CS+ > CS− and specific PIT contrasts S1/S2 > ITI) and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise activation results (FWE-corrected) with maps deposited on NeuroVault. Participant demographics (age/sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and preprocessing/software (SPM12) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking cues (smoking vs neutral) and reports within-participant smoking>neutral contrasts. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and reported (one-sample t-test and flexible factorial Time x Stimulation interaction with voxelwise thresholds and cluster extent), with results presented as clusters/figures/tables. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age/sex summary, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM12). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task map, or non-empirical) apply. Thus it meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures of spicy vs non-spicy foods). It reports a within-subject contrast (spicy food > non-spicy food), and group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses with FWE correction and cluster thresholds; results are reported with coordinates and regions. Methods report participant age/sex, cue type (food), 3T scanner field strength, and processing software (SPM12). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual drug (cocaine) and natural reward (sexual) cues in 22 male cocaine patients. The paper reports within-participant, group-level voxelwise contrasts (cocaine vs. neutral; sex vs. neutral) analyzed with SPM2 and whole-brain voxelwise maps (thresholding and peak MNI coordinates provided and figures described). Participant age/sex, cue types, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM2) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for fMRI whole-brain cue-reactivity studies and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical).

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) visual cue-reactivity in healthy adults. It reports within-subject contrasts (high-hedonic foods > utilitarian foods; utilitarian > neutral objects), performs group-level whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses using SPM2 with FDR correction, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and violates none of the exclusions.

This study is not an fMRI cue-reactivity study. It is a tDCS neuromodulation trial measuring subjective appetite, cognitive and motor outcomes in people with obesity; no task-evoked fMRI data, whole-brain activation maps, or within-participant fMRI contrasts (e.g., food cues > control) are reported. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria requiring fMRI cue-reactivity (I3) and whole-brain task activation results (I5/I4). It is excluded on the basis that it does not provide the required fMRI activation data for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (chocolate pictures) contrasted with neutral control pictures and measuring cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (chocolate > neutral) and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise regression analyses (cluster-level correction via Monte Carlo simulation) linking BOLD responses to subsequent chocolate intake. Methods report participant demographics (female undergraduates), cue type (food/chocolate), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). Results are presented as whole-brain ROIs/clusters and described in text and figures/tables. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria and none of the exclusion criteria.

Although this is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (I3) and reports whole-brain task-evoked maps (interoceptive attention contrast) and participant information (I5, I7), it does not report any within-subject fMRI contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (no group-level, whole-brain cue-reactivity map for smoking images). The smoking pleasantness task is reported behaviorally, but no task-evoked smoking cue > control activation map is provided for inclusion in a cue-reactivity meta-analysis. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I4 and is excluded. Closest matching exclusion criterion: E4 (study emphasizes seed/resting connectivity and other task contrasts but lacks the required whole-brain cue-reactivity activation map).

This is an fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses and reports necessary methodological details. However, the manuscript does not report any within-participant contrast of reward cues > control stimuli (e.g., appetizing food > unappetizing food or food > non-food) at the whole-brain level. The reported analyses focus on instructional reappraisal contrasts (e.g., costs of eating > imagine eating) rather than a cue-reactivity contrast of palatable food versus control. Because it lacks a reported within-subject cue>control task activation contrast, it fails to meet inclusion criterion I4 and therefore cannot be included in the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food videos). The paradigm includes within-participant contrasts of food-video epochs versus control-video epochs. Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps using SPM2 with voxel-level FWE correction and cluster reporting; results/coordinates are reported (Table 2/ROIs). Relevant details are provided: participant age and sex (women, 18–45), cue type (food), scanner field strength (4-T), and processing software (SPM2). The study is empirical and not ROI-only or connectivity-only. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study reports a task-based fMRI investigation of methamphetamine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures) and reports a within-participant contrast of Methamphetamine Cue > Control Cue. Results are presented as whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (cluster-corrected Z>2.3, p<.05) with coordinates/tables and figures. The manuscript reports participant age/sex, cue type (methamphetamine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). All required inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study that measured cue-reactivity to e-cigarette cues using visual stimuli (images of e-cigarettes) and olfactory stimuli. Within-participant contrasts were performed comparing sweet-paired, nicotine-paired, and sweet+nicotine-paired cues versus control e-cigarettes, and group-level statistical maps were reported. The paper includes whole-brain analyses (and reports whole-brain-corrected correlations for liking) in addition to ROI/SVC results. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex, cue types (nicotine and sweet/food-related), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM12). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual presentation (participants held a cigarette and viewed a live video feed), includes a within-participant cigarette>control contrast (paired t-test/manipulation check), and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-correction. Methods specify 3T scanner and processing software (AFNI, FSL, etc.), and participant demographic/sex information are described. Whole-brain results are described in the text/tables. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

This study does not meet the review’s core topic inclusion criteria. It is an fMRI study of monetary reward/punishment processing (using colored-circle cues predicting gains/losses), not a cue-reactivity study presenting drug-related or natural reward–related visual cues (nicotine, food, sexual, etc.). Therefore it fails I3 and I4 (no drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity nor within-participant contrasts of drug/natural cues>control). Although whole-brain voxelwise analyses and participant details (age/sex, scanner field strength, AFNI processing) are reported (I5/I7), the primary content does not address visual drug or natural reward cue-reactivity required for the meta-analysis objective, so it must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study of food (natural reward) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie vs objects/low-calorie) and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses. However, the manuscript does not report the MRI scanner field strength in the provided full text, and inclusion criterion I7 requires reporting participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software. Because scanner field strength is not reported, not all required inclusion criteria are satisfied, so the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (nicotine) as distractors during a working memory task. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing smoking-cue trials to control (no-distractor) trials and neutral cues, using voxelwise whole-brain GLM and group-level repeated-measures ANOVA with cluster-correction. Results include tables and figures reporting coordinates and statistical maps (e.g., Tables 3–4, Figures 1–3) and report participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). Thus it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for group-level whole-brain task activation contrasts of drug cue>control using visual stimuli.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult never-smokers using visual food cues (natural reward). It includes within-subject contrasts of food>control pictures, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (exploratory whole-brain thresholds and tables/coordinates reported), and provides participant age and sex, cue type (food; nicotine manipulation reported), and MRI field strength (3T). Processing details are indicated (use of PickAtlas and first-/second-level GLMs; full processing details appear referenced in Supplementary Information). Therefore it satisfies the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies and reports whole-brain results and necessary participant/scanner information. Confidence slightly reduced because explicit naming of the primary fMRI processing software is not present in the main text but is likely provided in the supplement.

This is a task-based functional MRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue reactivity using visual picture stimuli. The study reports within-subject contrasts of smoking vs neutral cues and presents a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise ANOVA with FWE-corrected results and clusters described in the text/tables. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (AFNI, FSL for VBM, TBSS for DTI) are specified. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not resting/seed-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward). It includes within-subject contrasts of high-hedonic food images versus non-food objects (H>O), reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (voxelwise maps, FDR-corrected), and also provides figures/tables and methodological details. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This paper is a trial protocol describing planned fMRI cue-reactivity methods in smokers but reports no empirical fMRI results, statistical maps, coordinates, or within-participant contrast outcomes. The meta-analysis requires original task-evoked whole-brain activation data or reported coordinates from an empirical study. As a non-empirical protocol without original fMRI data in healthy adults, it meets exclusion criterion E6 (non-empirical paper without original fMRI data). Although the protocol specifies visual smoking cues and planned within-subject contrasts (negative smoke > positive smoke), no results are provided for inclusion.

This study is an fMRI task study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-participant contrasts (fattening>objects, nonfattening>objects, fattening>nonfattening). The paper reports group-level voxelwise (whole-brain) exploratory analyses and corrected Z statistic images and provides anatomical coordinates/locations; whole-brain results are described in the text and supplemental materials. Participant age range and sex distribution are reported, cue type is food, and processing/analysis methods (e.g., Local Analysis of Mixed Effects, Harvard-Oxford atlas) are described; scanner acquisition and processing details are referenced in the Supplementary Methods (acceptable per criteria I6). Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for fMRI visual natural reward cue-reactivity and reports whole-brain task-evoked results suitable for meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Participants passively viewed food images and the authors report a within-participant contrast of food cues > control (nature) images. Subject-level contrasts were submitted to a second-level, group T-map (whole-brain voxelwise analysis). Methods report participant age and sex (75 female college students, ages 18–23), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T Philips), and processing software (SPM8). Although an IFG ROI was examined, whole-brain group results were generated and used in analyses, so the study is not ROI-only. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met.

This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward cues (food) in adults and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software. However, the paper’s analyses are exclusively seed-based task-dependent connectivity (PPI) comparing oxytocin vs placebo; it does not present group-level whole-brain, task-evoked activation maps or within-participant contrasts of high-calorie cue > control stimuli as whole-brain voxelwise results needed for inclusion. Although a prior publication from the same dataset reported BOLD activation, those whole-brain task maps are not provided in this manuscript. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only seed-based/effective connectivity without accompanying whole-brain task activation maps (E4).

Full text was not accessible (site content failed to load), so I cannot verify whether this study meets critical inclusion criteria. The title suggests an fMRI cue-reactivity study in heroin users, but the provided content does not confirm: I3 (use of visual stimuli), I4 (within-subject contrast drug cues > control), I5/I6 (reporting of whole-brain, group-level voxelwise results or coordinates), or I7 (reporting of participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software). Because ALL inclusion criteria must be verifiable and at least one could not be confirmed from the provided information, I must exclude the study. If the full text or supplemental materials become available, re-screening could be done.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food stimuli (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts comparing high-calorie (food) vs low-calorie/control images (e.g., snack go vs vegetable go). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using FSL (Z>2.3, cluster p<0.05 corrected) and results are reported with coordinates, figures, and ROI follow-ups. Relevant metadata are provided: participant age and sex, cue type (food), 3T scanner field strength, and processing software. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state only, includes whole-brain task activation maps). Thus all required inclusion criteria (I3–I7) are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study examining cue-reactivity to visual natural rewards (sexual images) and monetary rewards. The manuscript reports within-subject contrasts comparing Sexual (CS+Sex) versus Neutral (CS−) stimuli and outcomes, with group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (whole-brain FWE cluster-corrected results and MNI coordinates are reported). Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (SPM8) are specified; participant sex and age characterization and cue type (sexual) are described. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual tobacco (e-cigarette) advertising cues to assess cue-reactivity in young adults (nicotine-related stimuli). It reports within-subject contrasts comparing flavored/tobacco e-cigarette images versus control images, and includes group-level whole-brain analyses (cluster-corrected results with coordinates referenced in supplement). Participant age and sex information, cue type (e-cigarette/nicotine), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are all reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI visual cue-reactivity studies and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study that uses visual drug-related cues (images of cocaine/crack/IV/non‑IV) versus neutral images (water) within a within‑subject design. The GLM explicitly models drug-incentive anticipation events and contrasts (premature vs. correct within drug trials) and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (cluster-forming z>2.3, p<.05) including drug‑condition activations and SDI>HC group comparisons. The paper provides coordinates, figures, and ROI/whole-brain results, and reports participant demographics (age/sex), cue types, scanner field strength (Siemens TIM Trio 3T), and processing software (FSL, SPM12). Thus it meets the review’s requirements for an fMRI visual cue‑reactivity study with within‑participant contrasts, whole‑brain results, and necessary methodological details.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual sexual cues (pornographic vs. neutral images) and reports within-subject contrasts (explicit>neutral) and group-level, whole-brain random effects analyses (FWE-corrected clusters). Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (sexual), MRI scanner (3T Siemens Skyra) and processing software (SPM12) are reported. Results include whole-brain activation maps and coordinate tables and also report PPI connectivity but not exclusively. Thus all inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) using visual stimuli and includes within-participant contrasts of hedonic > neutral foods, and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). However, the analyses are ROI-based (a priori ROIs with mean values extracted) and the authors explicitly note they did not perform a whole-brain analysis due to sample size. No group-level, voxelwise whole-brain statistical map is reported. Thus the study violates the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

Although this is an fMRI study using visual cues to elicit a natural reward (juice) and reports participant age/sex, scanner strength, and processing software, the task analyses are ROI-based (caudate and putamen) with extracted regional activity used for downstream analyses. The manuscript does not present a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or coordinates for a cue>control contrast; results focus on ROI measures and RSFC. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain task activation evidence and meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results).

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (visual) cues with detailed participant demographics, 3T scanner, and processing software reported; however, the primary group-level whole-brain neuroimaging contrast reported and described is Attend CS+ versus Regulate CS+ (an emotion regulation/reappraisal contrast). The manuscript does not report a whole‑brain, group-level task contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (CS+ > CS−) suitable for cue‑reactivity meta-analysis, even though behavioral CS+ vs CS− effects are reported. Because inclusion criterion I4 (at least one within‑participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli in the neuroimaging domain) is not met, the study must be excluded from the cue‑reactivity meta-analysis despite meeting other requirements.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food) with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie vs. nonfood objects; low-calorie vs. nonfood objects) and group-level random-effects GLM analyses. Whole-brain thresholding and voxelwise results are reported (Talairach coordinates, cluster-level statistics, FDR correction); results are presented in text/figures. Relevant methodological details are reported: cue type (food), MRI field strength (4.0T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX); participant demographics are summarized in the Participant Characteristics (group sizes, BMI, handedness) and demographic reporting is implied in text/tables. Therefore the study meets I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 for inclusion in the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This fMRI study used visual monetary incentive cues (monetary gains/losses) rather than drug-related cues or the natural reward cue types listed in the meta-analysis (e.g., food or sexual). Although it includes within-subject cue>neutral contrasts, reports whole-brain group-level voxelwise analyses, and provides participant age/sex, scanner (3T) and processing software (SPM8), it does not assess drug or the specified natural reward-related cue types required by I3. Therefore it fails to meet inclusion criterion I3 and must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and reports group-level whole-brain task maps and participant details. However, it does not include a within-participant contrast of natural reward cues > non-reward control stimuli: both main conditions are food images (calorie-labeled vs. unlabeled), so there is no food>neutral (or equivalent) contrast required by inclusion criterion I4. Therefore the study fails to meet all required inclusion criteria and must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual cues (written labels). The study reports within-subject contrasts (e.g., high-calorie > low-calorie; labels vs neutral) during anticipation and response phases, and performs group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses (cluster-level FWE correction). Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Thus it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and violates none of the exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue reactivity using visual stimuli. The manuscript reports within-participant contrasts (food > non-food and high-calorie > low-calorie), and presents group-level whole-brain random-effects analyses with cluster-level correction (Monte Carlo) and reported significant clusters. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age range (25–70), mixed-sex sample (sex referenced), cue type (food/high- vs low-calorie), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager 20.6). Results include whole-brain correlation analyses and cluster findings (described in text), satisfying the criterion that whole-brain results can be reported in text/tables/figures. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the review.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual sexual stimuli (penile-vaginal penetration), a natural reward cue type ('sexual'), with within-subject contrasts (e.g., PEN>NEU, PEN>BOD). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (flexible factorial second-level models; FWE-corrected maps), processed with SPM8 on a 3T scanner. Participant sex and ages are reported (adult women). Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate any exclusion criteria (no ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical limitations).

This is an fMRI study using visual sexual cues (natural reward-related) and reports within-subject contrasts of sexual>neutral backgrounds (main effect of background). Analyses were performed as whole-brain, voxelwise SPM12 group-level statistics with FDR correction and reported coordinates/figures/tables. Participant age and sex (male), cue type (sexual), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM12) are all reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, and is empirical).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural rewards) and reports within-participant contrasts (food [high+low] vs nonfood, high-calorie vs low-calorie, high-calorie vs nonfood). Group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) analyses are reported (within-group and between-group whole-brain results and figures/tables described). Necessary methodological details are provided (participant age/sex, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3.0-T, processing software: SPM-12). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects, empirical data in humans). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for fMRI visual natural reward cue-reactivity and should be included in the meta-analysis.

The study is an fMRI food cue-reactivity investigation and meets several inclusion criteria (visual food cues, within-participant contrasts, group-level voxelwise analyses with reported coordinates). However, the report as provided does not include key required participant and methods details per I7 — participant age is not reported and processing software is not explicitly stated (only implied via marsbar/‘as previously described’). Because all inclusion criteria must be met and I7 is not clearly satisfied in the provided text, the study must be excluded.

This is a within-subject fMRI study using visual sexual (pornographic) cues to assess cue-reactivity and inhibitory control. The manuscript reports a within-participant contrast of pornographic > neutral images, performs group-level whole-brain analyses (cluster-level FWE correction reported), and provides relevant participant information (age, all-male sample), cue type (sexual), and processing software (SPM12). ROI analyses are also reported but whole-brain voxelwise task effects are present, and results for the pornographic vs. neutral contrast are described in text and figures/tables. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI studies of natural reward (sexual) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural rewards) with event-related presentation of food vs visually matched non-food images and analyses contrasting food>non-food. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (cluster-corrected Z>2.3, p<0.05) with tables/figures describing results. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T) and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore the paper meets all required inclusion criteria for whole-brain task-evoked visual cue-reactivity studies and violates no exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue reactivity in adolescents using visual alcohol picture stimuli. The analysis includes within-subject contrasts of alcohol>non-alcohol beverage pictures and reports group-level voxelwise whole-brain results (exploratory whole-brain 3dttest++ with cluster-corrected significant clusters). Relevant methodological details are reported (participant age/sex distribution, cue type = alcohol, MRI field strength = 3T, processing software = AFNI). Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for visual stimulus task-evoked whole-brain cue-reactivity fMRI studies.

This is an fMRI study of smoking cue reactivity using audio/visual stimuli with within-subject contrasts (SC > non-SC). However, all reported task analyses were conducted within an a priori ROI mask (including dorsal/ventral striatum and other regions) rather than as an unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level task activation map. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only ROI-constrained results with no whole-brain results (E3).

This is a task-based fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The study reports within-participant contrasts of food > object (food cues vs control stimuli). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are described (whole-brain cluster-corrected results, FLAME/randomise), and results are reported in text and supplementary materials. Participant demographics (age range, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (FSL/Freesurfer) are reported. The study also includes ROI and PPI analyses but does not rely solely on ROI or connectivity-only results. No exclusion criteria are met. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual smoking cues with within-participant smoking>nonsmoking contrasts, but the paper reports ROI-based parameter estimates and subsequent ROI-focused regression/mediation analyses. The full text does not report or describe group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or coordinate tables/figures of whole-brain results. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results without whole-brain results).

This fMRI study examines neural responses to visual food cues (natural rewards) and reports Enhance>Regulate and Regulate>Enhance within-subject contrasts using palatable food images. However, it does not report a standard within-participant contrast of natural reward-related cues > control (e.g., food images > neutral images); the primary reported contrasts compare task conditions (Enhance vs Regulate) rather than cue vs control. Additionally, reported neuroimaging results rely on region-of-interest small volume correction analyses rather than an unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level task activation map. Thus the paper fails to meet I4 and I5 and falls under E3 (only ROI analyses reported). Relevant study details (age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, processing software) are reported. Consequently the study is excluded.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with within-subject food>scenery contrasts. Group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses and t-maps are reported, with peak coordinates and cluster-level correction; an interaction map comparing ghrelin vs control groups is also presented. Participant demographics (age, sex: healthy adult males), cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5T), and analysis software (fmristat) are reported. The study does not rely solely on ROI or connectivity analyses. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity whole-brain fMRI studies of natural rewards.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The study reports within-subject contrasts of food > nonfood stimuli, performs group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) analyses with cluster-level correction, and provides relevant methodological details (participant ages and sexes, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: BrainVoyager). Results are described at the whole-brain level (voxelwise F/t/r maps and cluster results). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task maps, or non-empirical) are violated. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study is an fMRI investigation of cigarette purchase decision-making (a neuroeconomic purchase task), not a cue-reactivity task presenting drug-related visual cues versus control stimuli. It does not include any within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control stimuli or natural-reward cues > control stimuli; instead it contrasts decision types (Inelastic/Elastic/Suppressed) during a purchase task. While the paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise activation maps (satisfying I5 and acceptable reporting per I6) and provides participant age/sex and processing software, it fails the core inclusion criteria I3 and I4 (no visual cue-reactivity paradigm, no cue>control contrast). Therefore it must be excluded.

This is an fMRI food-cue reactivity study, but cues were delivered as personalized guided-imagery/audio scripts (auditory/internal imagery), not visual stimuli. Therefore it fails Inclusion Criterion I3 (use of visual stimuli). The study does include within-participant contrasts (favorite-food > neutral), reports whole-brain, voxelwise group results, and provides participant/cue/scanner details, satisfying I4, I5, I6, and I7. No exclusion criteria E3–E6 apply, but failure to meet I3 makes the study ineligible for the meta-analysis.

This paper reports mindfulness intervention RCTs with resting-state fMRI, DTI, and behavioral measures of emotion regulation and smoking reduction. It does not assess drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, provides no within-participant contrasts of drug-cues > control stimuli, and does not report a whole-brain task-evoked activation map for cue-reactivity. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (resting-state/task-free analyses only without a whole-brain task activation map).

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual alcohol images in healthy adults. It reports a within-participant contrast (alcohol > pleasant) and presents group-level whole-brain task-evoked results, including cluster coordinates for the left caudate (alcohol > pleasant). Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is alcohol, and full MRI acquisition and processing details are indicated to be in the Supplementary materials. Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for visual drug-related cue-reactivity and provides whole-brain results suitable for meta-analysis.

The study used an fMRI alcohol taste-cue paradigm (oral alcohol vs. water tastes) rather than visual drug or natural-reward cue stimuli, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I3 specifying visual stimuli. It does include a within-participant Alcohol Taste > Water Taste contrast (I4), reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise results (I5/I6), and provides participant demographics, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3.0T) and processing software (FSL) (I7). However, failure to use visual stimuli is sufficient for exclusion from the review focused on visual cue-reactivity.

This is an empirical task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with a food vs. non-food picture task. Subject-level within-participant contrasts for food and nonfood stimuli were computed and whole-brain voxel-wise group analyses were conducted (resulting in reported clusters/ROIs and statistics). MRI acquisition/preprocessing and analysis details are reported in the manuscript and supplementary materials, and participant age/sex and cue type are provided. Thus all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity visual-stimulus whole-brain fMRI studies are satisfied.

This is an empirical fMRI study examining neural cue-reactivity to food (a natural reward) using visual stimuli (milkshake images vs water images). It includes within-participant contrasts of milkshake picture > water picture and milkshake taste > tasteless, and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses using mixed-effects modeling in FSL (cluster-corrected results and cluster details reported). Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T GE), and processing software (FSL/FEAT) are reported. These elements satisfy inclusion criteria I3–I7. No exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study using food (natural reward) visual stimuli with whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses and adequate reporting of participant demographics, scanner (3T) and processing software (SPM8). However, the reported contrasts focus on healthy food choice vs healthy food reject and attention-condition comparisons (HD vs ND, TD vs ND) rather than a within-participant cue>control-stimulus contrast (e.g., food cues > neutral/control images) required for cue-reactivity meta-analysis. Therefore inclusion criterion I4 is not met. No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) are triggered: whole-brain results are reported and this is an empirical task activation study. Because at least one required inclusion criterion is missing, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (palatable food images) with within-participant contrasts (palatable food > neutral food and palatable food > non-food items). Group-level, whole-brain, voxelwise second-level analyses were performed (t-maps and F-maps across the cortical volume) with family-wise error correction, and coordinates/results are reported. Relevant details are provided: participant age group (adolescents/young adults), reported sex analyses, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state-only, and it is empirical). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for natural reward-related cue-reactivity visual fMRI studies.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food). It reports a within-subject contrast of high-calorie versus low-calorie food images (natural reward-related cues>control), includes a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) exploratory analysis (p<0.05 FWE) in addition to ROI analyses, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (SPM8). Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity meta-analysis and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject food>nonfood contrasts, and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with coordinates and corrected results. Participant age and sex (young women), cue type (food), MRI scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are all reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of food > nonfood (animal) images. Group-level, random-effects, voxelwise analyses were conducted in BrainVoyager with whole-brain contrast maps reported (figures/contrast maps and cluster-level correction described). Relevant participant demographics (age 15–20, female), cue type (food), MRI scanner field strength (3-Tesla Siemens Trio), and processing software (Brain Voyager QX v2.2) are reported. The report includes whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps/contrast results (described in text and figures) rather than ROI-only or connectivity-only analyses. Thus all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an empirical task-based fMRI study using visual natural reward stimuli (food images) with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie > non-food) reported at the group level. Analyses were whole-brain, random-effects voxelwise GLM with cluster-based correction and peak coordinates reported in tables/figures. Participant age and sex (older, majority female endometrial cancer survivors), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (BrainVoyager) are all reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity studies and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of cocaine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (images of cocaine and paraphernalia), with a within-subject contrast (cocaine pictures > neutral objects). Group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses were conducted and thresholded using SPM8 (voxelwise p<0.01, cluster-level FWE p<0.05), and results are reported regionally in the text (and supplemental figures). Relevant participant demographics (age range, sex), cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met.

This is an fMRI study of visual food cues (natural reward) using food images and matched non-food controls. It includes within-participant contrasts (viewing food vs. viewing non-food; distraction food vs. distraction non-food). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with whole-brain FWE cluster correction and reported peak coordinates and rendered maps. Relevant participant information (female sample, age-matching), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Thus all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an fMRI task-based study using visual alcohol cues (AFID task) in heavy drinkers and reports within-subject contrasts (alcohol vs neutral and alcohol vs food) during the anticipatory phase. The authors conducted voxel-wise, group-level whole-brain LME analyses (and ROI analyses) with reported cluster coordinates and volumes, satisfying whole-brain reporting requirements. The paper provides relevant methodological details: cue type (alcohol, food), scanner field strength (3T GE and Siemens), preprocessing/analysis software (AFNI), and participant demographic information (age, sex referenced). Although the study includes a medication manipulation (varenicline vs placebo) and reports interaction effects, it also reports main task effects (alcohol>neutral) across participants, which meets the requirement for a within-participant drug-cue>control contrast. No exclusion criteria are triggered (whole-brain results are present, not ROI-only or resting-state).

The study is an fMRI investigation of visual reward cues (monetary: £5, £1 vs neutral) and reports within-participant contrasts (£5-neutral, £1-neutral). It provides group-level, whole-brain voxelwise results (FWE cluster-corrected maps and coordinate peaks) and accompanying ROI/PPIs, and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). These elements satisfy the meta-analysis requirements for cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and whole-brain reporting.

The paper does not report task-evoked drug- or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity contrasts. Although an alcohol cue-reactivity task was performed, its results are reported in a parent study and not in this full text; the presented analyses focus on emotional face (fear/anger) processing (faces vs shapes). Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I3 and I4 (no within-participant drug cue>control or natural reward cue>control contrast reported). No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) specifically apply, so exclusion is based on unmet inclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs neutral images), with a within-subject contrast (alcohol > neutral) reported. Group-level, whole-brain task-evoked results are presented (alcohol>neutral activations in mOFC, occipital cortex, PCC, SFG) and whole-brain analyses (voxelwise rsults and contrasts) are described. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is alcohol, scanner field strength (3T) is specified, and preprocessing/analysis details are referenced in the Supplementary Methods (satisfying reporting of processing pipeline/software). Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for visual drug-related cue-reactivity fMRI with whole-brain group results and requisite participant and methods information.

The study is an fMRI investigation of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports within-subject smoking>control contrasts. However, the second-level analyses were performed within predefined anatomical region-of-interest (ROI) masks (Harvard-Oxford atlas) and results are reported for those ROIs (FWE-corrected). There is no indication of an unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level activation map being reported. Therefore the study reports only ROI-based task activations and lacks whole-brain task-evoked results required by the meta-analysis. This violates inclusion criterion I5 and meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results).

This study used PET ([18F]fallypride) to measure dopamine release in response to cocaine cues, not functional MRI. The meta-analysis requires fMRI studies of cue-reactivity with visual stimuli and whole-brain task activation maps. Here the imaging modality is PET, analyses are ROI-based BPND comparisons (and correlations/mediation), and no group-level whole-brain voxelwise fMRI activation maps are reported. Therefore it does not meet the required fMRI modality or whole-brain activation criteria.

The study used a gustatory (taste) alcohol cue paradigm (alcohol vs juice) rather than visual stimuli, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I3 (visual stimuli). The paper does include a within-participant alcohol>control contrast (I4), reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise activation maps (I5), and provides participant age/sex, cue type, MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5) (I7). Because the primary review requirement is cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, this study is excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study that used visual drug-related cues (alcohol- and cannabis-related words) during task-activated fMRI and computed within-subject contrasts (alcohol>color, cannabis>color). Analyses were whole-brain voxelwise using SPM8 with cluster correction and second-level group analyses reported, and results are described in the text (with supplementary materials referenced). The paper reports participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol, cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task maps, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cocaine-cue videotapes (visual stimuli) and healthy and cocaine-dependent participants. Within-participant contrasts comparing tape viewing epochs to baseline (emotion 0 minus baseline 1; emotion 2 minus baseline 2) and cocaine-versus-other-tape contrasts are reported. Analyses are whole-brain, voxelwise, group-level (randomization procedure across voxels; voxelwise significance, figures/tables of activations are presented). Participant age/sex, cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM 96) are reported. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria and violates no exclusion criteria.

The paper is a narrative review/discussion summarizing results from a series of ERP and fMRI studies rather than reporting a single original fMRI experiment with new whole-brain, group-level task activation maps. The full text does not provide methods, scanner field strength, processing software, or voxelwise whole-brain statistical maps/coordinates for a within-participant drug-cue>control or reward>control contrast. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E6 (non-empirical paper without original fMRI data).

This is an empirical fMRI study of reward-related cue reactivity using visual anticipatory cues (monetary reward/loss cues) in a within-subject design. The study reports within-participant contrasts (Reward Cue–Neutral Cue and Loss Cue–Neutral Cue), performs group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FLAME) with significant clusters reported, and provides supplemental tables/figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (monetary reward), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL v5.0.6) are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI studies of natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

This is an fMRI task study using visual cue videos (cocaine, gambling, sad) that assesses cue-reactivity. Within-subject contrasts of video viewing versus baseline (V1>B1, V2>B2) are reported, including random-effects, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FWE-corrected) with tables/figures and coordinate reporting. Participant age/sex, cue types (cocaine and gambling), 3T scanner field strength, and processing software (SPM5, AFNI/BioImage Suite) are all reported. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for task-based, whole-brain visual cue-reactivity reporting and does not meet any exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only or connectivity-only reporting).

Although this is an fMRI study of natural reward (favorite-food) cue-reactivity with whole-brain, voxelwise analyses and reported participant/cue/scanner details (meets I4, I5, I6, I7), the task used personalized audiotaped guided-imagery scripts (auditory stimuli) rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I3), which is not met. Therefore the study must be excluded.

This JoVE neurofeedback study reports results from four nicotine-dependent participants trained to downregulate bilateral insular activation during craving-eliciting cues. However, the manuscript appears to be a targeted rtfMRI neurofeedback protocol and presents findings focused on the insula (region-targeted training) with a very small sample (N=4). There is no clear indication in the available full-text summary that a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map (drug-cues > control) was reported or that whole-brain statistical maps (or coordinate tables/figures) are provided. Therefore it does not satisfy the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked results (I5/I6) and appears to report ROI-focused analyses only. Exclusion is based on E3 (only ROI analyses, no whole-brain results).

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual smoking stimuli in adolescents. It includes within-participant contrasts of smoking cues > neutral cues, reports group-level whole-brain (second-level random-effects) voxelwise analyses with thresholds and cluster results (t-values/clusters), and provides necessary methodological details (participant age/sex, cue type = nicotine, 3T scanner field strength, SPM5 processing). Results are reported in the text with t-statistics and cluster locations. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity- or resting-state-only, and not non-empirical). Therefore all inclusion criteria are met.

The study is excluded because it uses individualized auditory guided-imagery scripts (audiotaped personalized stress, neutral, and favorite-food cues) rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis requires cue-reactivity studies using visual stimuli (I3), so this key inclusion criterion is not met. The study does meet other criteria: it includes within-participant contrasts (favorite-food vs neutral) (I4), reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses with FWE correction (I5/I6), and provides participant, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software details (I7). However, failure to satisfy I3 necessitates exclusion.

This is an empirical fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue-reactivity using visual images. The authors created beta maps contrasting trials with a smoking image versus two neutral images and used these in multivariate (searchlight MVPA) analyses. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise accuracy maps were produced, warped to MNI space, smoothed, and subjected to group-level statistical tests (including interactions with smoking group) with correction for multiple comparisons. Participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8, AFNI, FSL) are reported. Results include whole-brain voxelwise findings described in the text and figures/tables. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met (within-subject smoking>neutral contrast used as input, and group-level whole-brain analyses reported). No exclusion criteria apply.

Although this is an fMRI study using alcohol-related visual cues (meets I3) and a within-participant alcohol vs neutral contrast was performed (I4), the reported results focus on psychophysiological interaction (PPI) seed-based connectivity analyses. The simple univariate contrast (alcohol vs neutral) was reported as not significantly different and the paper does not present or report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked activation map or coordinates for task activation. Therefore the study reports only seed-based/effective connectivity results without an accompanying whole-brain task activation map, which matches exclusion criterion E4.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (alcohol images vs nonalcohol control images) and reports a within-subject contrast comparing alcohol>control (e.g., CRAlcohol-CRControl and GOAlcohol-GOControl). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted and reported (FWE-corrected results, cluster tables and figures), satisfying whole-brain evidence requirements. Relevant participant details (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), MRI scanner field strength (3.0T Siemens Trio), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. The study therefore meets all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity meta-analysis and does not violate any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

Full text was not accessible (page failed to load), so I cannot verify critical inclusion criteria. The title suggests a study of food craving but the available information does not confirm (a) that this is an fMRI study of visual cue-reactivity, (b) that it includes a within-participant contrast of food cues > control stimuli, or (c) that it reports group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps and required methodological details. Because the review requires explicit whole-brain task activation maps (I5) and reporting of participant/cue/scanner/software details (I7), and these cannot be confirmed from the limited record, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

The study examines visual alcohol cue-reactivity and reports within-participant contrasts (alcoholic vs. non-alcoholic beverages) and relevant participant/scanner/method details. However, all second-level voxelwise task analyses were spatially restricted to an anatomically defined mesocorticolimbic mask (i.e., not an unmasked whole-brain group-level voxelwise map). The review’s I5 requires at least one group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map. Because the paper does not present an unmasked whole-brain task activation map (only masked/regionally restricted results), it fails I5 and meets exclusion criterion E5 (no accompanying unmasked whole-brain task effect). Therefore the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

Full text was not accessible, so I cannot verify that this study used an fMRI visual cue-reactivity paradigm, reported a within-participant cue>control contrast, or provided group-level whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked results and required methodological details. The title emphasizes striatal reward sensitivity, which suggests an ROI-focused analysis rather than whole-brain reporting. Because key inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5, I7) cannot be confirmed and the study likely reports ROI-based findings without whole-brain maps, I exclude it. Low confidence reflects uncertainty due to missing full-text information; if full text is obtained, re-screening is recommended.

This fMRI study used visual monetary cues (reward vs neutral) in a within-subject design and reports group-level task contrasts (reward>neutral) and whole-brain analyses (GLM thresholding described). Participant age/sex, cue type (monetary reward), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Although a small-volume correction was applied for the NAcc ROI, the study performed group-level whole-brain GLM and reports whole-brain PPI results; results and coordinates are clearly described. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements for fMRI cue-reactivity using visual natural reward stimuli, within-participant contrasts, whole-brain task maps (described in text/figures), and necessary methodological details.

This is an empirical fMRI study using a monetary incentive delay task with visual cues predicting monetary gain, loss avoidance, or neutral outcomes — fitting the review’s scope of natural reward-related (monetary) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The study reports within-participant contrasts (e.g., gain or loss-anticipation/avoidance vs. neutral/none). Whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted and described (results reported with regional activations), and relevant participant/cue/scanner/method information is reported in the paper. Therefore it meets the required inclusion criteria for cue type (monetary natural reward), within-participant contrasts, whole-brain task activation reporting, allowance for coordinate reporting, and reporting of key study details.

This study is a task-based fMRI investigation using visual alcohol cues in abstinent alcoholics vs abstract control stimuli. It reports a within-participant contrast (alcohol-associated > abstract stimuli) and presents group-level, voxelwise whole-brain results (SPM99 fixed-effects group analysis, reported activations/coordinates). Participant age and sex are provided, cue type is alcohol, scanner field strength (1.5 T) and processing software (SPM99) are explicitly reported. The study is empirical and not ROI-only, nor connectivity- or resting-state-only. Therefore it satisfies the meta-analysis requirements for inclusion.

This is an event-related task fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues with a within-subject contrast of drug cues > neutral stimuli. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM8) on data acquired at 3.0 T and results are reported as regional activations (between-group and within-group contrasts). Participant sex (male) and cue type (heroin) are reported, along with scanner field strength and processing software. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This study used an fMRI cannabis cue-exposure task with visual stimuli and reported within-participant contrasts (cannabis cue > appetitive cue) and participant details (age, sex, cue type, scanner, processing software). However, the reported results are derived from region-of-interest (ROI) extractions (mean z-statistics within predefined ROIs) rather than a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. The paper does not present unmasked whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps or whole-brain results (only ROI-based analyses), so it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and meets exclusion criterion E3.

This is an fMRI study of visual drug (cigarette) and natural reward cue reactivity with within-subject contrasts and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses; however, the manuscript does not report the MRI scanner field strength (Tesla) in the methods. Inclusion criterion I7 requires relevant reporting of participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software. Because scanner field strength is not reported in the provided full text, I7 is not satisfied, so the study cannot be included.

The study is an empirical task-based fMRI investigation using visual cocaine-related cues versus natural scene control images, with within-subject drug-cue>control contrasts and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses reported. Methods specify a cue-reactivity visual paradigm, 3T scanner, and preprocessing/statistical analysis in FSL. Results describe whole-brain voxelwise activation maps (group-level contrasts) and report coordinates/cluster results and correlations with abstinence. Participant age and sex (male, mean age reported), cue type (cocaine), field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are provided. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not resting/seed-only, and is empirical). Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is a task-based fMRI study of smokers using visual (videotaped) cigarette cues (nicotine) with within-subject contrasts (cigarette cue > neutral and resist vs crave). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (cluster thresholding, FSL/FEAT/FLAME), and results are described in text (with coordinates/cluster locations). Participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Thus all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study using visual anticipatory cues for monetary rewards (natural rewards) in substance-dependent patients and controls, with within-subject contrasts of reward cues > non-incentive cues and loss cues > non-incentive cues. Group-level whole-brain, voxelwise analyses were performed and reported (AFNI; 3dANOVA2) with cluster-corrected maps and VOI analyses; results are presented in the text and tables/coordinates. Participant age/sex, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for task-based whole-brain cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

Although this is an empirical fMRI study of food (natural reward) cue-reactivity with within-participant contrasts and whole-brain task activation maps reported (meeting I4, I5, I6, I7), it does not meet I3 because the cues were delivered as personalized auditory guided-imagery audiotapes rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires visual stimulus-based cue-reactivity studies. Therefore the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of cocaine using script-guided imagery (auditory/imagery) rather than visual stimuli, so it fails inclusion criterion I3 (requires visual stimuli). Additionally, the report does not provide all required methodological/participant details per I7 (participant age and processing software are not reported in the provided text, though sex, cue type, and scanner field strength [1.5T] are). The study does include a within-participant drug-cue versus control (stress) contrast and reports whole-brain voxelwise activation clusters (meeting I4, I5, I6), but failure to meet I3 (and I7) is sufficient for exclusion from the meta-analysis.

This study is an fMRI cue-exposure experiment using visual heroin-related and pleasant images in abstinent opioid-dependent males and healthy controls. It reports within-participant contrasts (heroin vs. baseline/neutral; pleasant vs. baseline), group-level random-effects whole-brain analyses with voxelwise results and coordinates, and regression analyses. Methods report participant age and sex, cue type (heroin and pleasant), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM2). Results include whole-brain activation maps and coordinates (reported in text/tables) meeting meta-analysis requirements. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) are violated. Therefore the study satisfies all inclusion criteria for visual drug and natural reward cue-reactivity fMRI studies.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol (drug) and food (natural reward) cue reactivity using visual cue symbols and a task that contrasts alcohol cues against neutral/food cues. The paper reports within-subject task contrasts (e.g., high-threat alcohol cues vs high-threat food/neutral cues and alcohol-cue conditions) and group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses conducted in AFNI with cluster correction. Participant age/sex information, cue types (alcohol, food, neutral), scanner field strength (Siemens Prisma 3T), and processing software (AFNI) are all reported. The study therefore provides task-evoked, whole-brain activation maps and the necessary methodological details for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of active cocaine users using visual drug-related cues (cocaine pictures) and neutral controls, reporting within-subject contrasts (cocaine-related > neutral) and whole-brain, group-level voxelwise activation maps (voxelwise thresholds and cluster correction). The paper provides participant age/sex, cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI). Thus it meets all inclusion criteria and does not violate exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cannabis cues that assessed cue-induced craving and reported within-subject contrasts (cannabis cues > Gaussian baseline, cannabis > nature, cannabis > food). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM5 with cluster-level inference and activated clusters reported; results are described in text and clusters provided. Participant age/sex, cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T Philips), and processing software (SPM5) are all reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This article is a narrative review/opinion piece summarizing fMRI and addiction literature rather than an empirical fMRI study. The full text contains no original methods, participants, experimental design, within-participant cue>control contrasts, or group-level whole-brain statistical maps/coordinates produced by the authors. It discusses prior fMRI findings and potential interventions but does not report new task-evoked whole-brain results using visual cue-reactivity. Therefore it does not meet the inclusion criteria for an original fMRI cue-reactivity study and meets the exclusion criterion for non-empirical papers without original fMRI data.

This is an empirical fMRI study that used visual alcohol cues and neutral control pictures and reports within-subject contrasts (alcohol cues > neutral). Group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses are reported (one- and two-sample t-tests with coordinates and cluster results), and results are presented with tables/coordinates and SPM2 processing details. Participant age and sex (male, mean ages), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) are all reported. The study does not rely solely on ROI or connectivity analyses and includes the requisite whole-brain task activation maps/coordinates. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and violates no exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (nicotine) with within-participant contrasts (smoking cue > neutral cue) and reports whole-brain task-evoked results (exploratory whole-brain analyses with cluster-level FWE significance). Participant age and sex (male only), cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM12, CONN, FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria for task-based cue-reactivity studies using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cue-reactivity stimuli (tobacco, money, neutral) in nicotine-dependent smokers. It includes within-subject contrasts comparing tobacco cues to neutral and to monetary cues, and reports group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses (disjunction mask, voxelwise one-sample t-tests with cluster correction) with reported clusters/ROIs. Relevant participant demographics (age, all-male sample), cue types, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Results are described with identified clusters (and supplementary materials noted), satisfying reporting requirements. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study comparing smokers and non-smokers and reporting ROI-based intra- and inter-network coupling and correlations with ADHD symptoms. It does not include any task-based cue-reactivity paradigm using visual drug or natural reward cues, nor does it report within-participant contrasts of drug-cue > control stimuli or any group-level whole-brain task-evoked activation maps. Therefore it does not meet key inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5) and falls under exclusion for studies reporting only resting-state/seed/ROI analyses without whole-brain task activation results. Exclusion criteria applied: E4 (resting-state/seed-based connectivity only) and E3 (ROI-only analyses with no whole-brain results).

Although this study is an fMRI investigation of natural reward (juice) using a visual conditioned cue and reports within-subject event contrasts (CS vs other event types) and relevant participant/scanner/method details, the reported analyses are focused on a priori ROI-based statistics. The full text describes voxel-wise preprocessing but presents group results and statistical testing primarily as ROI mean values rather than unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level activation maps or coordinate tables/figures of whole-brain task effects. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked results (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

This is an fMRI study of emotion processing (emotional faces task and dot-probe) in AUD participants and does not use drug-related or natural reward-related visual cues (e.g., alcohol images) nor report any within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli. Although the paper reports group-level, whole-brain task activation maps and provides coordinate tables and methods (satisfying I5 and I6), it fails to meet the core inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis: I3 (task must assess drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli) and I4 (must include a within-participant drug-cue > control contrast). Therefore the study is ineligible for inclusion in this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study with visual drug-related words and whole-brain analyses, but it does not report any within-participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (analyses were collapsed across word type because no word-type effects were observed). The meta-analysis requires at least one within-participant drug-cue>control or natural-reward>control contrast (I4), which is not present. The study does meet other requirements (visual stimuli, whole-brain task maps, and reporting of participant demographics, cue type, scanner and processing software), but absence of the critical drug-cue vs. control within-subject contrast precludes inclusion.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (appetitive food images) with a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control stimuli.
