The paper reports a group-level, whole-sample one-sample t-test (whole-brain, voxelwise, FDR-corrected) demonstrating task-evoked activation, and also provides ROI and between-group analyses. Relevant methodological details are reported (cue type: food; MRI field strength: 3T; processing software: SPM5; participant groups and sex matching). Although the group comparison focused on an a-priori ROI, the presence of an unmasked, whole-brain task effect satisfies the whole-brain evidence requirement. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis objective.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual video stimuli, reporting within-participant contrasts of smoking versus control videos. The paper describes group-level, mixed-effects whole-brain voxelwise analyses (t-maps) with correction and lists significant peaks (Tables S1–S2) and figures, fulfilling whole-brain evidence requirements. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (NIAK, fmristat) are specified. Results are not limited to ROI-only analyses and a within-subject drug>control contrast is explicitly tested and reported. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria and does not violate exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study in daily smokers that uses visual cues (picture representations of money, cigarette puffs, or neutral) during a rewarded guessing task and reports within-subject contrasts comparing smoking cue anticipation (puff) > neutral and money > neutral. Group-level, whole-brain (exploratory) analyses are reported (uncorrected p<.001, cluster thresholds) in addition to ROI analyses. Key methodological details required by the meta-analysis are present: participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8). Thus the study meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate listed exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes task activation maps).

Although this fMRI study examines nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and within-subject SC>non-SC contrasts and reports participant age/sex and scanner/software details, all task analyses were conducted within an a priori ROI mask and results are reported as ROI-restricted voxelwise clusters and ROI beta extractions. The paper does not present an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Therefore it reports only ROI-restricted task results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, reporting within-subject contrasts of high-calorie vs. low-calorie cues. Whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses were performed with SPM5 (cluster-corrected maps reported), and relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software are provided. All required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of insula functional connectivity after 12 h smoking abstinence, not a task-based cue-reactivity experiment. It does not employ visual drug or natural reward cues, nor report any within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli or any task-evoked, whole-brain group-level activation maps. Instead, it reports seed-based resting-state connectivity analyses. Therefore it does not meet the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies and violates exclusion criterion E4 (resting-state/seed-based connectivity without task activation).

Although this study uses visual alcohol cues and reports within-subject cue>control fMRI contrasts (I3, I4), it is a single-case report (one patient) and does not provide a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map as required by I5. Key methodological details required by I7 (e.g., MRI scanner field strength and processing software) are not clearly reported in the provided text. Because it lacks the required group-level whole-brain activation maps, it does not meet the meta-analysis inclusion criteria and must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual drug-related stimuli (drug words) in cocaine-addicted participants. The study includes within-participant contrasts comparing drug versus matched neutral words (drug>neutral and drug<neutral contrasts were created), and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using random-effects SPM2 (voxelwise t-tests and coordinates reported). Reporting includes voxel coordinates and descriptions in the text and figures. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (cocaine), MRI scanner field strength (4T), and processing software (SPM2) are all provided. Although the drug>neutral difference did not survive cluster-level correction, the inclusion criteria do not require significance, only that the within-subject contrast and whole-brain group analysis were performed and reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This study is an fMRI investigation of cognitive control (a multisensory numeric Stroop task) in individuals with cocaine use disorder, not a drug or natural reward cue-reactivity task using visual cues. It does not include any within-participant drug-cue > control-stimulus contrasts or report cue-reactivity to visual drug/natural reward stimuli. Although whole-brain task-evoked results and methodological details (scanner field strength, AFNI processing, participant age/sex) are reported (I5, I6), it fails to meet key inclusion criteria I3 and I4 (no drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity and no drug-cue > control contrast). Therefore it is not eligible for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (geometric figures) in a monetary incentive delay task to probe anticipation of monetary reward and loss (natural reward-related cue-reactivity). The paper reports within-participant contrasts (gain cues > neutral cues; loss cues > neutral cues) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM5, with reported coordinates and cluster statistics; small-volume correction for the ventral striatum was applied but whole-brain results and coordinates are provided. Participant sex and age-matching, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study reports a functional MRI experiment using visual heroin-related cues in early-abstinent heroin users (meets I3). It includes within-participant contrasts of heroin-related vs neutral stimuli and group-level analyses (heroin-related > neutral; meets I4). Analyses were whole-brain, voxelwise random-effects SPM2 tests with reported MNI coordinates (meets I5 and I6). The paper reports participant age and sex, cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) (meets I7). It is empirical fMRI task activation data and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task maps).

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual cues (gaming screenshots and smoking images) that reports within-participant contrasts (gaming>neutral and smoking>neutral) and group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses using SPM5 (3T scanner) with corrected thresholds and conjunction analysis. Participant sex (males) and age criteria are reported. Results are reported as whole-brain activations and described in the text/tables/figures. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met, and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study of cocaine cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (cocaine-related words) with a within-participant cocaine>neutral contrast. The paper reports group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation analyses (FWE-corrected), describes significant clusters and between-group contrasts, and refers to supplementary materials for results. Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (CamBA/SPM) are reported, and participant age/sex and cue type (cocaine) are provided. Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

This is an empirical 3T BOLD fMRI study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual smoking vs neutral pictures, reporting within-participant contrasts (smoking cues > neutral). The paper presents voxelwise, whole-brain group-level analyses (cluster-corrected results with coordinates reported for the mPFC and individual peak coordinates) and details preprocessing and SPM5 software. Participant age, sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software are reported. It therefore satisfies inclusion criteria for an fMRI visual cue-reactivity whole-brain task activation study and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (drug-related heroin pictures and water-related pictures) and reports within-participant contrasts (Drug > Neutral; Thirst > Neutral). Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise SPM2 second-level one-sample t-tests with voxel-wise thresholds and cluster extent; results are reported with Talairach coordinates and cluster information. Participant age/sex, cue types, scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2) are all reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task effects). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related (cigarette) cues and matched neutral images, addressing cue-reactivity (I3). Within-participant Craving>Neutral contrasts are reported for each group and used in analyses (I4). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-correction, coordinates, and t-values for Craving>Neutral and between-group contrasts (I5, I6). Methods include participant demographic information, cue type (nicotine/cigarette), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX), fulfilling I7. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

Although the study presents fMRI CBF responses to cocaine-related videos (visual drug cues) and thus meets I3, it does not report a within-participant task contrast of drug cues > control stimuli nor a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked activation map. The analyses are primarily seed-based connectivity (amygdala–dorsal cingulate) using wavelet transform coherence and individual-level ASL connectivity measures. These are connectivity-focused results rather than unmasked whole-brain task activation maps required for inclusion. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (only seed-based/connectivity analyses without a whole-brain task activation map).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of hedonic food cues > nonfood objects. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with a whole-brain cluster-corrected threshold and reported MNI coordinates/figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met.

The paper is an fMRI study of practice effects on a sustained attention/incentive task in cocaine users, not a cue-reactivity study. It does not present analyses of drug- or natural reward-related visual cue reactivity (I3 not met) and, in this secondary analysis, monetary conditions were averaged such that there is no within-participant contrast of reward cues > control stimuli reported here (I4 not met). The study does report whole-brain, group-level voxelwise SPM analyses and provides participant demographics, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength, and processing software (meets I5, I6, I7). Exclusion is therefore due to failure to meet required inclusion criteria I3 and I4.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward (food) cues with a within-subject contrast of appetitive > neutral stimuli. Group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) random-effects GLM analyses and activation maps were reported (cluster thresholds and brain regions described). The paper reports participant sex and age matching, stimulus type (food images), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results are not limited to ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI studies using visual natural rewards are met.

This is an event-related fMRI study that used visual reward-predicting cues (money and cigarette images) and measured cue-elicited anticipatory activity and trial-by-trial behavioral responses. Within-subject contrasts (parametric increase in reward level; reward vs. 0-level) were modeled for both cigarette and monetary cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted using SPM5 (3T scanner) with reported thresholds and anatomical coordinates. Participant characteristics (sample sizes, age comparability, smoking status) and cue type (nicotine) are reported. Thus the paper meets all specified inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward cue-reactivity using visual food stimuli in healthy fasting adults (n=27). The study includes a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control images and reports group-level, whole-brain task-evoked activations (food vs control) with voxelwise results and coordinates. Relevant methodological details are provided (age, sex distribution, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM5). Although the primary personality correlation used small-volume ROI correction, the paper nevertheless reports whole-brain main effects and exploratory whole-brain analyses, satisfying the whole-brain evidence requirement. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This paper is a trial design/methods report that does not present empirical fMRI activation results or whole-brain task activation maps. Crucially, the planned cue paradigm uses personalized auditory imagery scripts (audio cues), not visual stimuli, so it fails the review's visual-stimulus requirement (I3). Although the protocol includes within-subject alcohol>neutral contrasts and reports scanner field strength and processing software, the primary planned fMRI analysis is PPI (seed-based psychophysiological interaction connectivity), and no unmasked, whole-brain task-evoked activation maps are reported or presented. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for studies that only report seed-based/effective connectivity without whole-brain task activation (E4) and is a methods/design paper without original fMRI activation data (E6).

This study does not meet the review’s core inclusion criteria. It used PET imaging of mu-opioid receptor binding rather than functional MRI, and it did not employ a visual cue-reactivity task or any within-participant drug-cues>control-stimuli contrast. Although voxelwise PET binding maps were analyzed, there is no whole-brain, task-evoked fMRI activation map from a cue-reactivity paradigm. Therefore criteria I3, I4, and I5 are not satisfied. The study is empirical and reports neuroimaging data, but it lacks original fMRI task activation data required for this meta-analysis.

This is not an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual drug or natural reward cues. The study uses arterial spin labeling (PCASL) to measure cerebral blood flow during rest and a spatial working-memory task, not presentation of drug/food/sexual visual cues. There is no within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli or natural reward-cues > control stimuli. Although whole-brain perfusion and connectivity analyses are reported, they do not constitute task-evoked, visual cue-reactivity contrasts required by the review. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria I3 and I4 and does not provide the required whole-brain task activation maps for cue-reactivity. Exclusion criterion applied: E4 (no relevant whole-brain task activation map for cue-reactivity; study focuses on connectivity/rest-task-rest perfusion rather than cue-reactivity).

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study in chronic smokers using visual stimuli (aversive and appetitive smoking images and matched neutral images). The study reports within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control (aversive smoking-related vs neutral; appetitive smoking-related vs neutral) and group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (one-sample t-tests) with cluster correction; results are described in text and supplementary tables/figures. Required metadata are reported: participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM5). Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking and neutral images, reporting a within-participant contrast (smoking > neutral). The authors conducted a group-level, whole-brain mixed-effects (FLAME) analysis for the smoking vs. neutral contrast (cluster-corrected p<0.05) and describe the whole-brain activation and overlap with the DMN ROI. Participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T/Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study used a monetary incentive delay task probing neural responses to monetary reward anticipation and receipt in cocaine-dependent versus control participants. It is not a cue-reactivity study presenting visual drug-related or natural reward-related cues, and it does not include within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control stimuli or natural reward-related cues > control stimuli. While the paper reports whole-brain task activation results and provides coordinates and participant details (meeting I5, I6, and I7), it fails to meet core inclusion criteria I3 and I4 for this meta-analysis (visual cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards). Therefore the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study of visual nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity with within-participant smoking>neutral contrasts and whole-brain random-effects results, and reports scanner field strength and processing software. However, the provided full text does not explicitly report participant age information (only that 21 women were studied). Inclusion criterion I7 requires relevant information regarding participant age and sex; age is not reported in the provided material. Because not all inclusion criteria are met, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using pictures of personally-craved and not-craved foods. It reports within-subject contrasts (e.g., Look Craved > Look Not Craved and Look Craved > Look Neutral) at the group level. Whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked analyses are performed and reported (cluster thresholds, peak coordinates, cluster sizes) and small-volume relaxation for the ventral striatum is described. Participant demographics (ages 10–23, all female), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM12b, FSL BET, AFNI AlphaSim referenced) are all reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not only between-group contrasts). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of smokers using visual stimuli and reports within-subject contrasts (smoking>neutral, positive>neutral) with coordinates and methodological details. However, the group-level statistical tests were performed within an a priori anatomical mask (AAL regions), i.e., ROI-restricted voxelwise analysis rather than an unmasked, whole-brain group-level task activation map. Because the paper reports only ROI-masked results and no unmasked whole-brain task effect, it fails the review’s whole-brain evidence requirement and meets exclusion criterion E3.

This article is a symposium/review-style paper summarizing multiple human laboratory studies of stress and alcohol using guided imagery, IV alcohol self-administration, and clinical assessments. It is not an original fMRI study of drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli; the cue modalities described are primarily guided-imagery/audio and mood induction, not visual cues. The paper does not present a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) fMRI activation map, nor does it report scanner field strength or processing software for whole-brain fMRI analyses. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI-visual-cue and whole-brain analysis inclusion criteria and fits the exclusion of non-empirical/summary papers lacking original fMRI data.

This study is a resting-state, seed-based functional connectivity analysis comparing alcohol dependence and internet gaming disorder. It does not include a task-based cue-reactivity paradigm using visual stimuli, does not report any within-subject contrast of drug (or natural reward) cues > control stimuli, and does not provide whole-brain task-evoked activation maps. Therefore it fails the core inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI studies and meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only seed-based connectivity/resting-state analyses.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (high-calorie food images) and reports within-subject contrasts between cue conditions and passive viewing/baseline. A group-level whole-brain RFX ANOVA was performed and corrected whole-brain maps/fROIs are reported. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager) are all reported. Although neutral non-food control images are not used, the paper reports within-participant contrasts (e.g., up-regulation/suppression vs passive viewing and condition vs passive baseline) and whole-brain results, satisfying the review’s requirements for natural reward cue-reactivity with whole-brain task-evoked maps.

This study does not meet the core cue-reactivity inclusion criteria. Although it is an fMRI study with whole-brain results (and reports participant age/sex, nicotine cue type, 3T scanner, and AFNI processing), the task is a real-time smoking decision paradigm with stress manipulations and nicotine delivery rather than a visual cue-reactivity paradigm comparing drug-related visual cues versus control visual stimuli. There is no within-participant contrast of drug cues > control visual stimuli reported. Therefore inclusion criteria I3 and I4 are not met.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with within-subject contrasts (high-calorie foods>objects and low-calorie foods>objects). Group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted (they report whole-brain thresholding at P<0.001 FWE in addition to ROI small-volume corrections) and results are described in the text and figures/tables. Relevant methodological and participant details are provided (female participants, ages 19–28, food cue type, 3T scanner, SPM8 processing). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state only, includes within-subject contrasts and whole-brain task analyses). Therefore the study meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity using visual natural reward stimuli.

This is an fMRI study of smoking cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures). The authors report within-subject contrasts comparing smoking versus neutral pictures (LCSP minus LCNP) that index attentional bias/cue-reactivity. Analyses are group-level whole-brain random-effects GLM results reported voxelwise (between-group comparisons and one-sample main effects) with peak coordinates and Z-values provided. Scanner field strength (3T), processing software (SPM5), participant ages and sex distribution, and cue type (nicotine/smoking) are all reported. The paper does not rely solely on ROI, connectivity-only, or non-empirical methods. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria (I3–I7).

Although this study used visual food cues and within-participant contrasts (palatable vs non-palatable vs neutral) and reports scanner field strength and processing software, it does not provide usable whole-brain task-evoked results (no reported group-level whole-brain statistical maps or coordinates). The authors state that between-group voxel-wise analyses did not survive correction and that activation for each condition relative to baseline is not reported. The meta-analysis requires group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task maps or coordinates; absence of reported whole-brain results prevents inclusion.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (cigarette) cue-reactivity that used visual stimuli (dynamic smoking videos). It reports within-subject contrasts (smoking > neutral), conducted voxelwise whole-brain analyses (with reported peak coordinates and z-statistics), and provides participant age/sex information, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes task-evoked whole-brain maps). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the review.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (appetizing vs. plain) as natural reward stimuli in a within-subject food-choice task. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing appetizing and plain food cues and whole-brain, group-level voxelwise statistical results (FWE/FDR-corrected) with anatomical coordinates and supplementary tables. Key methodological details required by the meta-analysis (participant age/sex, cue type: food, scanner field strength 3.0T, and processing software SPM8) are provided. Results include both within-group and between-group whole-brain activations and correlations with behavioral craving measures, and whole-brain maps/coordinates are available in supplementary materials. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward stimuli (romantic/sexual images) with within-participant contrasts of romantic>neutral. Analyses used whole-brain, group-level random-effects voxelwise testing (SPM8) with cluster correction and report task-evoked activations across the brain. Participant age/sex, cue type (sexual/romantic), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software are reported. Thus it meets the meta-analysis requirements for visual natural reward cue-reactivity and provides whole-brain results suitable for coordinate-based inclusion.

This study reports an fMRI experiment using visual methamphetamine-cue pictures in methamphetamine-dependent participants and healthy controls. It includes within-participant contrasts (methamphetamine task vs rest baseline) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses conducted in SPM5 with an explicit voxelwise threshold and cluster extent. Participant age and sex, cue type (methamphetamine), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not resting-state/connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward). It reports within-subject contrasts of hedonic food images > non-food objects (and hedonic > neutral food), conducted at the group level with voxelwise whole-brain random-effects analyses using SPM5 and cluster-level correction. Results include whole-brain activations with coordinates and t-values, plus an ROI supplement; participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

This is an empirical fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using a within-subject high-caloric > low-caloric contrast. Analyses were whole-brain, voxelwise group-level SPM12 results with cluster-level FWE correction and reported cluster/coordinate information. Participant age and sex (male-only), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are all provided. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical). Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity that includes visual stimuli (high-ED vs low-ED food images). It reports within-participant contrasts of high-ED > low-ED, and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM8 with cluster correction and reporting of activation loci. Key methodological details required by the meta-analysis are provided: participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the review are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This fMRI study used visual smoking-related stimuli (tailored and untailored smoking-cessation messages) contrasted against neutral control messages within subjects (smoking-cessation messages > neutral), and the amygdala and other brain regions were analyzed. The paper reports voxel-wise, whole-brain exploratory results (coordinates, cluster extents, significance thresholds) in addition to anatomically defined ROI analyses. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine-related, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (SPM) are specified. Therefore it satisfies the meta-analysis requirements for visual drug-related cue-reactivity, a within-participant drug-cues>control contrast, whole-brain voxelwise results (reported in text/tables/figures), and reporting of required methodological/participant details.

This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cues with within-subject contrasts and relevant participant/cue/scanner details, but the neuroimaging results are reported using an a priori ROI approach with small-volume correction. The Methods state analyses were run within an ROI mask (WFU Pickatlas) and SVC in SPM8; no unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level task activation map or whole-brain correction is described. Therefore the study reports only ROI-based task results and does not provide the required whole-brain task-evoked statistical map, violating inclusion criterion I5 and meeting exclusion criterion E3.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (subliminal sexual images) with healthy adult participants (n=37, males). It reports within-subject contrasts of sexual cues > neutral (Sex vs Neu) and Sex vs Fix, and presents group-level whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps (one-sample t-tests and cluster results for Sex vs Neu) with reported clusters/local maxima. Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (FSL/FEAT) are reported, as are participant age/sex and cue type. Although some group differences were analyzed with an ROI mask, the study includes qualifying whole-brain results and coordinate/cluster reporting. Therefore it meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) and includes within-subject contrasts of high-energy food vs. neutral and low-energy vs. neutral. Group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM12 with a whole-brain FDR threshold and reported cluster results. Relevant methodological details are provided (cue type: food; MRI scanner: 3 T Siemens TRIO; processing software: SPM12), and participant sex is specified (female). Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity meta-analysis of visual natural rewards.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward cue-reactivity (food) using visual stimuli and reports within-subject contrasts of food>objects and whole-brain group analyses. However, the full text as provided does not report the MRI scanner field strength (required information per I7). Because all inclusion criteria must be met, and I7 (reporting scanner field strength alongside age/sex, cue type, and processing software) is not satisfied in the provided text, the study must be excluded. No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) are triggered.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual sexual images (a natural reward) as cues and neutral control images. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing sexual > neutral images for both the line-orientation (Sex LO > Neutral LO) and picture-categorization (Sex PC > Neutral PC) tasks. Group-level second-level analyses were performed (one-sample and two-sample t-tests) and the manuscript states that whole-brain analyses are reported in the online supplementary material, fulfilling the requirement for a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked map. Participant age and sex are reported (n=97, 47 men, 50 women; mean age 25.3±4.5), cue type (sexual), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM12) are all documented. Although ROI analyses are presented, whole-brain results are available; no exclusion criteria are met. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study used gustatory (taste) high-calorie cues delivered as liquids (personalized beverages) rather than visual cue stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires fMRI cue-reactivity studies using visual stimuli (I3). Although the paper includes a within-participant contrast of high-calorie cues > water (I4), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation maps (I5/I6), and provides methodological details (scanner 3T, FSL processing) (I6), the primary cue modality does not meet the required visual-stimulus criterion. Therefore it must be excluded.

This study used fMRI with visual food (natural reward) cues in children and reports contrasts of portion-size conditions, but the neuroimaging results are presented using an ROI approach rather than group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation maps. The manuscript states the authors previously published a whole-brain analysis on a subset, but the present full-cohort analyses relied on ROI contrasts and do not provide unmasked, whole-brain group-level statistical maps (voxelwise results) or coordinates for a whole-brain task effect. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only ROI analyses without whole-brain results.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (desserts and vegetables) assessing natural reward-related cue-reactivity. Subject-level contrasts included food cue conditions versus rest (nogo_dessert vs rest; nogo_vegetable vs rest), and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps were reported (pre- vs post-surgery difference maps; whole-brain thresholding reported). Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are reported. Although the primary group-level tests examine pre-post differences, the underlying within-participant cue>control contrasts and whole-brain group maps satisfy the meta-analysis requirements.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking cues (nicotine) and neutral/evocative images, reporting within-subject contrasts of smoking-related > neutral cues (within-group NAcc and amygdala activations). The paper includes group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (whole-brain corrected thresholds and cluster maps) and reports results in tables/figures and text. Methods report participant demographics (age and sex distribution discussed), cue type (smoking/nicotine), MRI scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI). Therefore all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity whole-brain fMRI studies using visual stimuli are met, and no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an fMRI study using visual monetary cues (rings of dollar/pound signs) to probe reward-related cue reactivity in abstinent smokers vs. non-smokers. The design includes within-subject contrasts of reward (cue) vs. neutral trials and reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (thresholding and cluster correction via AlphaSim) with reported peak coordinates and extracted time courses. Participant age/sex, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (FSL, AFNI) are reported. Thus the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It reports within-participant contrasts (food conditions modeled vs baseline and contrasts of availability and calorie content) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (reported at p<.005 uncorrected and ROI-corrected results), with coordinates provided. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Note: no separate non-food neutral image control condition was reported, but condition vs baseline and task contrasts were available at the whole-brain group level, supporting inclusion for a meta-analysis of visual natural reward cue-reactivity.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (package images) with within-subject contrasts comparing package/taste conditions (e.g., regular > healthier packages). The paper reports group-level voxelwise analyses (exploratory whole-brain analyses followed by ROI analyses) and provides cluster coordinates and statistical maps sufficient for meta-analysis reporting. Participant age/sex (34 females, ages 18–35), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are all reported. Although the main inferential results are presented within an ROI mask, the authors explicitly conducted exploratory whole-brain analyses and report voxelwise cluster results/coordinates, meeting the whole-brain reporting requirement. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity of natural rewards using visual stimuli.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with a within-participant contrast of alcohol > neutral cues, and reports whole-brain group-level voxelwise analyses (random-effects) with coordinates and thresholding. Processing software (SPM) is reported, participant sex is described, cue type is alcohol, and imaging protocol details are referenced in the manuscript/supplementary material. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity whole-brain fMRI studies.

This is an empirical within-subject fMRI study using visual erotic images (IAPS) as natural reward-related cues. The paper reports within-participant contrasts of erotic scenes > neutral control (task-evoked), and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (p<0.05 FWE) as well as ROI checks. Necessary methodological details are reported (participant age/sex: 55 healthy young men, scanner: 3T TRIO, software: SPM8) and cue type is sexual (natural reward). No exclusion criteria apply.

This study reports resting-state seed-based functional connectivity and correlational analyses with craving and BMI change. Although participants rated craving to food photos, those ratings were collected after the resting-state scan and no task-based fMRI cue-reactivity paradigm or within-participant cue>control contrasts are reported. No whole-brain task-evoked activation maps (cue-reactivity contrasts) are presented. Therefore the study does not provide task-based cue-reactivity whole-brain results and meets exclusion criterion E4.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual erotic stimuli (sexual cues) to assess reward-related cue-reactivity and the effects of GHB in a within-subject, placebo-controlled design. The paper reports within-participant contrasts (e.g., placebo/erotic > placebo/neutral and GHB/neutral > placebo/neutral) and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (thresholds reported, whole-brain and ROI analyses described). Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (sexual), scanner field strength (3 T), and processing software (SPM8) are all reported. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, contains whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts (HC–C, LC–C, HC–LC, Food–C) comparing responses after glucose vs water. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise statistics in SPM8 (voxel threshold and cluster correction described) on data acquired at 3.0 T. Participant sex (female/PCOS), age/demographics, cue type (food), scanner field strength, and processing software are reported. Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria for visual natural reward cue-reactivity, within-participant cue>control contrasts, whole-brain voxelwise results (reported as clusters/coordinates), and required reporting details.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual infant-face stimuli (natural reward-related cues) with within-subject contrasts (own vs. unknown infant faces) and whole-brain, voxelwise group analyses (SPM12; 3T scanner; voxel p<.001, pFWE<.05). The paper reports task-evoked whole-brain statistical maps (coordinates and clusters described) and references demographic information for participants, and reports processing software and scanner field strength. The within-participant contrasts (familiarity: own>unknown) constitute natural reward-related cues>control comparisons appropriate for inclusion. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without whole-brain task maps, or non-empirical) apply.

The study is an fMRI investigation using visual natural reward cues (food) with within-participant contrasts of high-calorie > low-calorie foods (HGo vs LGo) and reports participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). However, the paper's analyses are reported as ROI-based (parameter estimates extracted from predefined ROIs) and do not present group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or whole-brain coordinates in the full text (detailed GLM results are reported elsewhere). Thus it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cues using visual stimuli and reports within-participant contrasts (fattening>object, non-fattening>object) and participant/scan details; however, all group-level analyses are reported using a region-of-interest (14 predefined regions) approach with cluster-thresholding within ROIs. The paper does not report unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level task activation maps or whole-brain voxelwise results in tables/figures/supplementary materials. Therefore it fails the whole-brain evidence requirement and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

The study uses visual monetary reward cues (dollar signs) versus neutral stimuli in an fMRI antisaccade task, providing a within-subject contrast of natural reward-related cues > neutral. The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (3dLME in AFNI) with cluster correction and presents results and time-course analyses in the text/figures. Participant age/sex, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (3T Allegra), and processing software (FSL, AFNI) are all reported. Thus it meets I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, and is empirical).

Although this is an fMRI study of nicotine cue-reactivity using visual smoking images and includes within-participant contrasts (smoke vs neutral) and ROI-based statistics (satisfying I3 and I4), the paper does not report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map. Analyses are based on individual, task-driven ROIs and group-level tests on ROI-derived percent signal change. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I5 and violates exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results). Additionally, sex of participants is not reported, so I7 is not clearly satisfied. Primary reason for exclusion: lack of whole-brain group-level activation maps (E3).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward). It includes within-participant contrasts (food vs. non-food and food vs. blurry baseline), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-level correction and Talairach coordinates, and provides participant age/sex, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). Thus it meets inclusion criteria I3–I7. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply.

The study is an fMRI investigation of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-subject contrasts (high-calorie vs cars; high- vs low-calorie) and reports participant age/sex, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM2). However, all inferential statistics were performed using an a priori ROI approach with small-volume correction; the authors do not report an unmasked, whole‑brain (voxelwise) group-level task activation map. Inclusion criterion I5 (at least one group-level whole-brain voxelwise map) is not met, and this study therefore falls under exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results with no whole-brain results).

This is a protocol/clinical trial design paper describing planned fMRI assessments of responses to visual food cues but does not report any original empirical fMRI results or present group-level, whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps. While it involves visual natural reward (food) cues (satisfying I3) and describes within-participant food>control contrasts (I4), it fails to provide reported whole-brain task activation results (I5) and contains no original fMRI data—thus meeting exclusion criterion E6 (non-empirical paper without original fMRI data).

This is a task-based fMRI study of natural (sexual/social) reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (anticipatory cues for opposite- vs. same-sex smiling faces). The design includes within-participant contrasts comparing reward cues (face trials) to control trials (non-facial stimuli) and analyses of anticipated smile intensity. Group-level, whole-brain task-evoked analyses are reported (FWE-corrected whole-brain maps and exploratory whole-brain results with peak coordinates), and peak coordinates are provided. Participant age/sex, cue type (sexual/social faces), MRI field strength (3 T), and processing software (SPM5) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore the study meets all meta-analysis inclusion criteria.

This is a task-based fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity that used visual stimuli (also auditory) and reported within-subject contrasts (e.g., high-calorie vs low-calorie and food vs non-food), with group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM8) and corrected thresholds. Key methodological details required by the review are reported: participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software. Whole-brain results are described and presented (maps/tables/figures) and therefore suitable for inclusion in a visual cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

This study did not perform any fMRI data acquisition or report whole-brain, task-evoked fMRI results. It is a behavioral/methods paper developing and validating visual cue images and an fMRI paradigm (prototype), using self-reported VAS craving in adolescent inhalant users. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria I3 (must be an fMRI study), I4 (no within-participant fMRI contrast reported), and I5/I6 (no group-level whole-brain statistical maps or coordinates). It is best classified as a methods/validation paper without original fMRI activation data, matching exclusion criterion E6 (methods-only/non-fMRI original-data).

This is an fMRI study of visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts (HC–C, LC–C) and thorough reporting of participants, scanner (3T) and processing (SPM8). However, the reported analyses are an ROI-based approach (WFU Pickatlas masks, small-volume correction) and the manuscript does not present an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Because the paper reports only ROI/small-volume corrected results and lacks whole-brain voxelwise group maps, it fails inclusion criterion I5 and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results). Therefore it must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This study is a task-based fMRI investigation of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food pictures). It reports within-participant contrasts comparing food versus neutral object conditions at the group level (RFX ANOVA and t-contrasts/conjunctions). Analyses are whole-brain/voxelwise (F- and t-maps) with reported Talairach coordinates and fROIs; results and thresholding are described in the text and figures. Participant demographics (female, mean age ~19.3), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T Siemens Allegra), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX v1.9) are all reported. The study is empirical and not ROI-only, connectivity-only, or between-group-only. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI studies of natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward-related (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food words). It reports within-subject contrasts of food>neutral (food-fneu) at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise random-effects analyses (SPM8) on data acquired with a 3T scanner. Participant age/sex information, cue type (food), scanner field strength, and processing software are reported. Results include whole-brain activation maps and described contrasts (with thresholding and ROI/search-volume details) and supplementary material. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

The study is an fMRI go/nogo task examining predictive geometric-shape cues and ACC activation in methamphetamine-dependent subjects, not drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity. It does not include drug or natural reward visual cues or within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control or reward cues > control, so it fails I3 and I4 (and the cue-type element of I7). The paper does include group-level whole-brain task analyses (secondary whole-brain analyses) and reports coordinates and methods, satisfying I5 and I6. No exclusion criteria E3–E6 apply. Therefore the study must be excluded from this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs neutral pictures) in adult non-dependent drinkers. The study reports within-subject contrasts (alcohol > neutral) and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (one-sample t-test and whole-brain regressions) with voxel- and cluster-level thresholds and reports peak MNI coordinates and cluster results. Relevant participant information (adult participants, sex breakdown), cue type (alcohol), processing software (SPM), and imaging protocol/supplementary materials are provided. The analyses include whole-brain task-evoked maps and coordinate reporting. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (beer images) as NoGo stimuli vs non-alcohol control images (water/coke). The design modeled Go and NoGo trials separately and produced group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) activation maps (SPM8) with reported thresholding; results describe alcohol-cue-related activations (insula, DLPFC, ACC) and comparisons between groups. Participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements for visual cue-reactivity, a within-participant cue condition (beer NoGo) contrasted with control stimuli representations, whole-brain group maps, and requisite reporting details.

This paper is primarily a theoretical/review article with preliminary interleaved TMS/fMRI data in cocaine-dependent participants. The fMRI analyses reported are TMS-evoked BOLD responses pre- versus post-cTBS, not visual cue-reactivity contrasts. The paper does not report a within-participant drug-cues > control-stimuli whole-brain task activation map from a visual cue-reactivity paradigm. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (sexual) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It includes within-participant contrasts of cues>neutral (CUE SEM – CUE NEUTRAL) and stimulus>neutral (SEM – NEUTRAL). The paper reports both ROI and explorative whole-brain analyses (family-wise error corrected whole-brain results reported), with whole-sample group-level task-evoked maps described in text and figures. Participant age and sex are reported (n=70, ages and sexes detailed), cue type (sexual), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Prisma), and processing software (SPM12) are all provided. Therefore it meets the review’s inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity studies and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, contains whole-brain group activation maps).

This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of cue-induced desire for an Internet video game using visual stimuli. It reports within-subject contrasts of video game cues > neutral control stimuli, conducted group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-level FDR correction, and presents coordinates for significant clusters. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (Internet video game), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (Brain Voyager BVQX) are provided. The design and reported results directly map onto cue-reactivity for a non-drug natural reward (video gaming) using visual cues, meeting the meta-analysis objective. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state-only, between-group-only without task effect, or non-empirical) are met.
